title: QMA vs. QCMA and Pseudorandomness
speaker: Henry Yuen
abstract:
In quantum complexity theory, QMA and QCMA represent two different generalizations of NP. Both are defined as sets of languages whose Yes instances can be efficiently checked by a quantum verifier that is given a witness. With QMA the witness can be a quantum state, whereas with QCMA the witness must be a classical string. It is clear that QMA contains QCMA, but could there be a separation? In other words, can a quantum state prove more to a polynomial-time quantum verifier than a polynomial-length classical string?
In 2007, Aaronson and Kuperberg asked whether there is a (classical) oracle separation between QMA and QCMA. We show that such an oracle exists if a certain quantum pseudorandomness conjecture holds. Roughly speaking, the conjecture posits that quantum algorithms cannot, by making few queries, distinguish between the uniform distribution over permutations versus permutations drawn from so-called "dense" distributions. It turns out that this pseudorandomness conjecture is deeply related to another, seemingly-unrelated, question in theoretical computer science known as the Aaronson-Ambainis conjecture.
Our result can be viewed as establishing a "win-win" scenario: either there is a classical oracle separation of QMA from QCMA, or there is quantum advantage in distinguishing pseudorandom distributions on permutations.
This is joint work with Jiahui Liu and Saachi Mutreja. No special knowledge about quantum computation will be assumed for this talk.

proof_stages.label: QMA/QCMA setup and motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the conditional separation goal, defines QMA and QCMA as quantum verification classes with quantum versus classical witnesses, and motivates why separating them would show that quantum proofs certify more complex statements than classical proofs.

proof_stages.label: Oracle-separation framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reformulates QMA versus QCMA in a query/oracle model, recalls previous quantum-oracle and restricted classical-oracle separations, and identifies the graph components problem as the classical-oracle candidate.

proof_stages.label: Components problem and QMA protocol
proof_stages.summary: A graph oracle is defined as a union of colored matchings, with yes instances having two equal disconnected components and no instances being expanders, and a QMA verifier is given via a signed uniform superposition plus balancedness and edge-invariance tests.

proof_stages.label: Dense-permutation pseudorandomness conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The needed pseudorandomness assumption is developed from dense distributions on bit strings to dense random permutations, with motivation from classical dense-oracle results, no-quantum-speedup conjectures, the Aaronson-Ambainis influence conjecture, and a low-degree partial bound.

proof_stages.label: Hard-coding the best classical witness
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a small-query, small-witness QCMA verifier, the yes oracles are partitioned by classical witnesses, a largest witness class is selected, and that witness is hard-coded to obtain a proofless quantum query algorithm accepting many yes instances and rejecting no instances.

proof_stages.label: Permutation parametrization and entropy decomposition
proof_stages.summary: Yes graphs are parametrized by permutations specifying the vertex partition and matchings inside each component, the accepted yes region is pulled back to a high-min-entropy distribution over these permutations, and a decomposition lemma expresses it as mixtures with few fixed coordinates and dense remainders.

proof_stages.label: Pseudorandom replacement and planted-structure lower bound
proof_stages.summary: The dense parts of the permutation distribution are replaced by uniform ones using the conjecture, yielding graphs with only small planted structures, and a known lower-bound/reduction shows such structures do not help distinguish two components from one expander with subexponential queries.

examples.name: Local Hamiltonian problem
examples.note: The QMA-complete minimum-energy problem for succinct quantum many-body Hamiltonians is used to make QMA concrete and to motivate the physical significance of quantum witnesses.
examples.engagement_reason: he defines the Local Hamiltonian problem and states its QMA-completeness/physical relevance without working through a particular Hamiltonian or verification

examples.note: This QCMA-complete Hamiltonian reconfiguration problem illustrates how a classical proof can be a short sequence of local gates connecting two low-energy states.

examples.name: Problems where quantum proofs do not help
examples.note: Approximating set size, proving formula unsatisfiability, and distinguishing one-to-one from two-to-one black-box functions are cited as contrasts where quantum witnesses appear not to improve one-way verification.
examples.engagement_reason: he lists tasks such as set-size approximation, unsatisfiability, and one-to-one versus two-to-one functions as cases where quantum proofs do not help without analyzing them

passes.reason: These tasks are cited precisely as cases where quantum witnesses fail to give extra verification power.

examples.name: Aaronson-Kuperberg reflection oracle separation
examples.note: The known quantum-oracle separation using a hidden reflected state illustrates what an oracle separation can look like and why a classical-oracle version would be more compelling.
examples.engagement_reason: he works through the Aaronson-Kuperberg reflection oracle separation, describing the identity-versus-reflection oracle, the QMA witness/check, and the QCMA lower-bound intuition

passes.reason: The oracle separates QMA from QCMA using the identity versus a rank-one reflection, a minimal nontrivial unitary promise.
the identity-versus-rank-one-reflection unitary oracle has a collapsed highly regular spectrum and is used to separate QMA from QCMA relative to an oracle
The identity-vs-rank-one-reflection oracle is a highly degenerate unitary promise used to separate QMA from QCMA relative to a quantum oracle.
The identity-versus-rank-one-reflection oracle is a minimal highly regular near-identity unitary used to separate QMA from QCMA relative to a quantum oracle.
the promise uses the trivial identity unitary versus a rank-one reflection and serves as a quantum-oracle separation of QMA from QCMA
The identity-versus-rank-one-reflection oracle is a collapsed highly special unitary case used as an oracle separation against QCMA.
The Aaronson-Kuperberg oracle uses a rank-one reflection, a minimal nontrivial perturbation of the identity, to separate QMA from QCMA relative to a quantum oracle.
The oracle contrasts the identity with a rank-one reflection, a minimal nontrivial perturbation used to separate QMA from QCMA.
The Aaronson–Kuperberg rank-one reflection oracle gives an oracle separation against QCMA, with a one-query QMA versus exponential QCMA gap.

The identity-versus-rank-one-reflection oracle is a highly minimal quantum oracle used to separate QMA from QCMA relativizedly.

examples.name: Even-parity dense distribution
examples.note: The uniform distribution over even-parity strings makes the definition of delta-denseness concrete: the full string loses one bit of entropy while every proper coordinate subset is uniform.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes why the uniform distribution on even-parity strings is 1/n-dense by noting the one-bit entropy loss globally and uniformity on smaller subsets

passes.reason: The even-parity distribution is a codimension-one parity hyperplane with exactly one global entropy deficit and full coordinate symmetry.
the even-parity distribution is a highly symmetric codimension-one parity subspace that exactly attains the 1/n dense entropy deficit
